task: I’ve got a subcontractor dispute brewing over who’s on the hook for a damaged section, and I want to handle it the right way before it turns into a bigger headache. What should I gather and put in writing first so I’m not boxed in later?

turns.agent_response:
Before anything else, get the facts locked down in writing and with photos. You want to protect yourself on **scope, existing condition, responsibility, and notice**.

Gather and document:

1. **Pre-work condition**
   - Clear photos/video of the damaged section from multiple angles
   - Close-ups and wide shots showing location/context
   - Date/time stamps if possible
   - Note whether it was already damaged, patched, cracked, rotted, loose, etc.

2. **Who had control**
   - Who was on site when it happened
   - Who had access, staging, supervision, and control of the area
   - Any truck/material placement or other site access issues

3. **What was in place already**
   - Existing protection, shoring, bracing, temp supports, barriers
   - Who signed off that it was safe to disturb anything
   - What’s carrying the load right now, if anything

4. **Scope and responsibility**
   - Original subcontract scope
   - Any exclusions, allowances, or clarifications
   - Any changed conditions or hidden-condition issues
   - Whether the damaged section was part of their work or outside it

5. **Communication trail**
   - Texts, emails, field notes, daily reports, RFIs, photos
   - Any verbal instructions converted into written confirmation
   - Timeline of when the damage was first noticed and by whom

6. **Damage details**
   - What exactly is damaged
   - Whether it’s structural, finish, utility-related, or cosmetic
   - Any impact to adjacent areas, neighbors, dust/debris, or floor protection

7. **Cost and mitigation**
   - Temporary protection or emergency measures taken
   - Who authorized them
   - Preliminary repair estimate, if available
   - Whether anyone is claiming the damage is from pre-existing conditions

Best move: send a **short written notice now** saying:
- you’re documenting the condition,
- no one should alter the area further until it’s inspected,
- and responsibility is not being admitted by either side.

If you want, I can draft a tight **reservation-of-rights / notice-to-preserve** email you can send right away.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right. I’d want it tighter. Not just photos and a notice — I want the paper trail nailed down before anybody starts talking loose.

Get these in writing first:
- exact date/time the damage was found
- who first saw it
- who was working in that area right before it happened
- the subcontract number and scope language
- any daily report, delivery log, or inspection note tied to that section
- the condition before their crew touched it
- what temporary protection was in place and who removed or moved it
- any instructions I gave, and if they were only verbal, get them confirmed by email now
- witness names, even if they’re just foremen or laborers
- photos/video before anyone patches, braces, cleans, or covers anything

And one thing the answer didn’t really hit hard enough: don’t let them “fix” it first and argue later. That’s how people cook the record. Freeze the area if you can, send notice, and make them respond in writing who says what caused it.

If you want, draft me the notice in plain English, short and hard.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tight **plain-English notice** you can send now:

---

**Subject:** Notice to Preserve Condition / Damage Documentation

Team,

A damaged section has been identified at **[location]** on **[date/time]**.

Until this is inspected and documented, **do not patch, clean, cover, brace, remove, or alter** that area or any related material.

Please confirm in writing:

- exact date/time the damage was first found  
- who first observed it  
- who was working in that area immediately before it was found  
- the subcontract number and scope language tied to that section  
- any daily reports, delivery logs, inspection notes, or field records for that area  
- the condition before your crew touched it  
- what temporary protection was in place, and who removed or moved it  
- any instructions given, including verbal instructions, confirmed by email  
- witness names of anyone who saw the condition or the work in that area  
- whether you contend your crew caused the damage, and if so, how

We are documenting the condition now and **no responsibility is admitted** at this time.

Please respond in writing immediately.

---

If you want, I can also turn this into a **firmer version** that reads more like a claim notice and less like a coordination email.

user_simulator.text:
Better. Still a little soft.

I’d want it to say they’re not to touch a damn thing unless I approve it, and I want a deadline on the response. Also add that any repair work before inspection is at their risk, because otherwise somebody’s going to get cute and start “making it safe.”

Make it read like this:
- preserve the condition
- no alteration without written okay
- answer by end of day / within 24 hours
- identify everyone involved
- state their position on cause and responsibility
- all rights reserved

Keep it plain English, but harder. And if you can, give me a version that sounds like I’m serious without turning it into a lawyer parade.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a harder plain-English version:

---

**Subject:** Preserve Condition / Written Response Required

Team,

A damaged section has been identified at **[location]** on **[date/time]**.

**Do not touch, repair, clean, cover, brace, remove, or alter anything in that area without my written approval.**  
If anyone does repair work before the area is inspected and documented, that work is **at your risk**.

Preserve the condition as found.

By **[end of day / within 24 hours]**, send written confirmation of:

- exact date and time the damage was first found  
- who first saw it  
- who was working in that area right before it happened  
- the subcontract number and scope language tied to that section  
- any daily reports, delivery logs, inspection notes, or field records for that area  
- the condition before your crew touched it  
- what temporary protection was in place, and who removed or moved it  
- any instructions given, including verbal instructions, confirmed by email  
- witness names of anyone who saw the condition or work in that area  
- your position on cause and responsibility

We are documenting this now. **No rights are waived, and all rights are reserved.**

Respond in writing immediately.

---

If you want, I can make it even shorter and more aggressive, like a **field notice** you can send from your phone in under a minute.

user_simulator.text: That’s closer. Shorter would be better, but the guts are there. I’d use it with the names and date filled in. No soft language, no room for games. Good enough.
